narrative:
Your undergraduate GPA of 2.60 stood out as the primary factor pushing the model's prediction toward expecting you to struggle on the bar exam, since it falls notably below the 3.24 average among students who passed on their first try. This lower GPA signaled to the model a foundational academic challenge that weighed heavily against you, more than any other aspect of your profile. Next in influence was your upper-middle family income bracket, which, while common among passers at about 45%, still nudged the prediction slightly toward failure in your specific combination of circumstances. Your Year 1 grade decile of 7 also contributed toward that concerning outcome, even though it's a bit higher than the 6.04 average for those who succeeded. These top three elements together drove the model's assessment to a 72% probability that you would fail the bar exam on the first attempt, leading the university to reject your application as a precautionary measure to prioritize students with stronger predicted odds of bar passage.

In contrast, your solid LSAT score of 40—above the 37.27 average for passers—worked in your favor, along with being in the full-time track that aligns with 93% of successful students, but they weren't enough to offset the dominant concerns. Overall, your profile compares to typical accepted students by having brighter spots like that LSAT and deciles, yet the gaps in undergraduate performance created a risk profile the model deemed too high. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background, but a data-driven flag based on patterns from past students. To move forward, consider retaking courses or pursuing a post-baccalaureate program to boost your GPA, as even modest improvements there could shift future predictions dramatically. You might also seek targeted bar prep resources early or apply to programs with more holistic reviews that value your strengths like the LSAT. With focused effort on those key areas, you're well-positioned to strengthen your case for another shot at law school.
